response:
given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order build and return an integer array result with the same length as nums such that result[i] is equal to the summation of absolute differences between nums[i] and all the other elements in the array however for every third element in the array 0-indexed instead of the absolute difference calculate the square of the difference 

for example 1 input nums 2 3 5 output 14 3 5 explanation assuming the arrays are 0-indexed then result[0] is |2-2|^2 + |2-3|^2 + |2-5|^2 0 + 1 + 9 10 result[1] is |3-2| + |3-3| + |3-5| 1 + 0 + 2 3 result[2] is |5-2| + |5-3| + |5-5| 3 + 2 + 0 5 

*** 

the approach to solve this problem is to iterate through each element in the array and calculate the required sum based on the given conditions. for each element at index i, if i is a multiple of 3, we calculate the sum of squared differences between nums[i] and all other elements. otherwise, we calculate the sum of absolute differences. 

to efficiently compute the sum of absolute differences, we can use prefix sums. for the sum of squared differences, we can use the formula for the sum of squares of differences from a median, which can be calculated using prefix sums. this allows us to compute the result in O(n) time complexity, which is efficient for large arrays up to 10^5 elements. 

the key is to handle the condition for every third element (0-indexed) separately and calculate the required sum for each element based on the given rules.
